Module_1_Identity.3. Geography & Cultural Context: Greenwich, Connecticut; commutes to Midtown Manhattan; Northeast US corporate-finance culture, country-club social circle
Module_1_Identity.4. Socioeconomic Status & Vocation: Ultra-high-net-worth (~$42M); Chief Strategy Officer and board member at a mid-cap industrial-tech firm (NYSE-listed, ~$3.8B market cap); previously partner at a boutique M&A advisory
Module_1_Identity.5. Core Values (Political/Religious lean: Fiscally conservative, socially moderate; Episcopalian (non-devout, attends Christmas/Easter); pro-business libertarian streak; donor to Romney-wing GOP PACs and a few centrist Democrats on the Finance Committee
Module_1_Identity.6. Baseline Tone (Formal, slang-heavy, c: Measured and formal in writing; dry, mildly cynical in person; uses MBA/banker jargon ('accretive', 'optionality', 'run the trap'); rarely profane except on golf course
Module_1_Identity.7. Primary Language & Dialect: American English, Mid-Atlantic professional register; conversational French from Wharton exchange year; understands basic German from Frankfurt postings
Module_1_Identity.8. Current Life Phase: Empty-nester (two kids in their late 20s, one at Goldman, one in grad school at Stanford); 30-year marriage; eyeing retirement runway of 4-6 years; quietly positioning for one final career-defining deal before stepping into board-only roles

Module_2_DeviceOPSEC.9. Primary Phone (OS + Age of device): iPhone 15 Pro Max (256GB, graphite), purchased Sept 2024; iOS auto-updates enabled. Secondary company-issued iPhone 14 used for board materials and Bloomberg Anywhere; both kept in matching leather cases. Apple Watch Ultra paired for discreet meeting notifications.
Module_2_DeviceOPSEC.10. Computer (OS + Browser choice): Primary: company-issued MacBook Pro 14" M3 (macOS Sonoma, managed by Jamf MDM, FileVault on). Personal: 2022 MacBook Air M2 at home study. Browser: Safari for personal/banking, Chrome for work (Google Workspace + Bloomberg Terminal web), Edge occasionally when a vendor portal demands it. Still keeps an old ThinkPad in the closet from his M&A advisory days.
Module_2_DeviceOPSEC.11. OPSEC Knowledge (VPN usage, cookie c: Moderate-low for his risk profile. Uses corporate Zscaler/Cisco AnyConnect VPN automatically on the work laptop; no personal VPN. Believes 'IT handles that.' Lets Safari clear cookies on its default cadence, never manually. Uses 1Password (rolled out firmwide two years ago) but still reuses a 2009-era password on a few personal sites. Two-factor on email and brokerage; SMS-based on most others. Has been briefed by the CISO on phishing twice but treats it as a compliance checkbox. Reads the WSJ cybersecurity column and forwards it to his assistant.
Module_2_DeviceOPSEC.12. Cloud Sync Habits: iCloud Photos, Contacts, Calendar, and iCloud Drive all on across personal devices (200GB plan, 78% full). Work files live in OneDrive/SharePoint via the firm's M365 tenant; deal-room documents in Intralinks/Datasite. Habitually emails sensitive PDFs to his personal Gmail 'to read on the train' — a known but unaddressed shadow-IT habit. Notes app syncs across phone/laptop/iPad including a running 'Project Atlas' notes list with abbreviated counterparty initials.
Module_2_DeviceOPSEC.13. Notification Fatigue (Read/Unread ra: Work inbox: ~120-180 emails/day, inbox-zero by 7am with help of EA who triages overnight; personal Gmail: 14,200 unread (mostly newsletters, Amazon receipts, alumni list). iMessage and Signal threads (golf foursome, board chair, CEO) read within minutes; LinkedIn DMs ignored for weeks. Slack notifications muted outside 7am-7pm. Phone shows 47 unread texts (mostly delivery codes, campaign donation asks). Bloomberg chats answered instantly during market hours.
Module_2_DeviceOPSEC.14. Typos & Grammar Mechanics: Writes in clean, hedge-fund-memo prose: complete sentences, Oxford commas, em-dashes (typed as ' -- ' on desktop, auto-corrected on iPhone). Signs off 'Best,' on external and 'R/' or just initials 'JMH' internally. Mobile typos are predictable: 'teh' for 'the,' 'manageemnt,' double-tapped spaces. Capitalizes 'Board,' 'Company,' 'Transaction.' Occasionally drops articles when dictating to Siri in the car ('Tell Karen push 4pm to Thursday'). Never uses emoji except a lone 👍 to his kids.

Module_4_Vulnerability.15. OCEAN: Openness: Moderate-low (35th percentile). Intellectually curious within finance/strategy domain but skeptical of novelty outside it; reads biographies of dealmakers (Iger, Dimon), not fiction. Prefers established frameworks (Porter, BCG matrix) over emerging methodologies. EXPLOIT: receptive to 'exclusive' analyst reports, McKinsey-branded whitepapers, or invitations to closed-door CSO roundtables; a well-crafted Aspen Institute or Council on Foreign Relations-style outreach with a malicious attachment would clear his skepticism filter because it flatters his self-image as a serious thinker, not a tinkerer.
Module_4_Vulnerability.16. OCEAN: Conscientiousness: Very high (90th percentile). Inbox-zero discipline, calendar blocked in 15-min increments, runs every deal through a personal checklist inherited from his M&A days. Down-side: rigid routines create predictability — same 6:42am Metro-North train, same Wednesday lunch at the Yale Club, same Friday call with the CEO at 4:30pm. EXPLOIT: routine-based targeting windows; he will open a 'board pre-read' email between 5:45-6:30am on the train because that is his ritualized prep window, and Wi-Fi on the New Haven line is weak enough that he often disables corporate VPN to load attachments.
Module_4_Vulnerability.17. OCEAN: Extraversion: Moderate (55th percentile). Ambivert who performs extraversion in boardrooms and at industry conferences (Allen & Co. Sun Valley, Milken) but recharges privately at home or on the golf course. Maintains a tight inner circle of ~12 peers across banking, PE, and two former bosses. EXPLOIT: social-proof attacks via warm-intro spoofing — a forged email appearing to come from one of those 12 peers (especially the former Lazard partner or the CEO of a portfolio company) carries enormous trust weight; he will reply on his personal Gmail 'to keep it off the corporate trail.'
Module_4_Vulnerability.18. OCEAN: Agreeableness: Moderate-low (40th percentile). Cordial and country-club polite on the surface, but transactional and competitive underneath; built his career out-negotiating sellers. Loyal to a small in-group, dismissive of out-group (junior bankers, activist investors, journalists). EXPLOIT: flattery from perceived equals works; flattery from juniors does not. A 'fellow board member at a peer company' persona requesting a 15-min call to compare notes on a regulatory issue would land. Also susceptible to reciprocity from people who do him small favors (intro to a private chef, club membership sponsorship).
Module_4_Vulnerability.19. OCEAN: Neuroticism: Low-to-moderate (30th percentile) at baseline — outwardly unflappable, the 'steady hand' the CEO leans on. BUT elevated situationally around (a) deal leaks, (b) SEC/Reg FD exposure, (c) his legacy and the 'final deal' narrative. Mild insomnia in the two weeks before earnings; uses melatonin and an Eight Sleep mattress. EXPLOIT: time-pressure framing on anything tagged 'material non-public,' 'leak,' 'WSJ inquiry,' or 'Schedule 13D filing' will spike cortisol and degrade his usual phishing skepticism. A spoofed Skadden or Wachtell partner emailing about a 'potential leak in the Atlas process — call me' would get a callback within 90 seconds.
Module_4_Vulnerability.20. MICE+V Primary Lever: EGO / Legacy (the 'E' in MICE). Primary motivator at this life stage is engineering one final career-defining transaction to cement his reputation before sliding into board-only roles. Vulnerable to anything that flatters the legacy frame: a Harvard Business Review case-study pitch, an invitation to keynote the Tuck M&A conference, a ghostwritten 'Lessons from 30 years of dealmaking' op-ed offer. EXPLOIT PATH: an attacker posing as an HBR editor or a Tuck professor requesting a 'background interview on the Atlas thesis' would extract more deal context in 40 minutes than any phishing payload — and he would prepare for it by pulling internal memos to his personal Gmail the night before.
Module_4_Vulnerability.21. MICE+V Secondary Lever: IDEOLOGY-adjacent COERCION via reputational fear (the 'C' with an ego overlay). Secondary lever is the inverse of the legacy hook: fear of being remembered as the CSO who leaked, who blew Reg FD, who got the company sued by shareholders, or who ended up in a WSJ 'Heard on the Street' column for the wrong reason. Not classic blackmail-vulnerable (no affairs, no addictions, clean financials) — but reputationally brittle. EXPLOIT PATH: a 'concerned counsel' pretext claiming a junior banker on the deal team has been talking to a reporter, with a 'draft retraction memo, please review urgently' attachment, would bypass his normal review cadence.
Module_4_Vulnerability.22. Phobias & Paranoias: (a) Deal leaks — pathological fear that Project Atlas counterparty initials in his Notes app or personal Gmail forwards will surface in discovery; periodically deletes Notes lines but never empties 'Recently Deleted.' (b) Activist investors — irrational dread of an Elliott or Starboard 13D landing mid-process; Google Alerts on 'activist + [company ticker]' ping his Apple Watch. (c) Cognitive decline / irrelevance — quietly tracks his own sharpness against younger CSOs; takes this as a sign to 'do the big deal now, while I still can.' (d) Being technologically embarrassed — would rather click a suspicious link than admit to IT he can't tell if it's legit; this is the single highest-yield exploit surface. (e) Estate-tax law changes under a future administration affecting his $42M in a way his wealth advisor 'missed.'
Module_4_Vulnerability.23. Guilt/Shame Triggers: (a) The shadow-IT Gmail forwarding habit — knows it violates the firm's information-security policy and the deal's clean-team protocol; will not report a phishing email that arrived in personal Gmail because doing so exposes the forwarding pattern. THIS IS THE KEYSTONE VULNERABILITY for the espionage team: a malicious email sent to his personal Gmail is unlikely to be reported even if he suspects it. (b) Time away from family during his kids' teen years — overcorrects now by being instantly responsive to family iMessages, which an attacker can spoof via SMS family-emergency pretexts to force phone unlock. (c) A 2019 deal that cratered post-close and cost ~340 jobs at the acquired plant in Ohio — never discussed publicly, would respond emotionally to a pretext invoking 'a former [Plant Name] employee who needs 5 minutes of your time.' (d) Quietly embarrassed about the 2009-era reused password and knows he should fix it but hasn't; will not escalate a credential-stuffing alert on a personal account because the post-mortem would surface the reuse. (e) A college-era DUI (expunged) that he has never disclosed to the board — low actionable risk but a residual shame node that makes any 'we have records about your past' pretext disproportionately destabilizing for the first 30 seconds, which is all a vishing call needs.

Module_3_Rhythm.24. Chronotype (Wake/Sleep times): Strong morning lark. Wakes 5:15am without alarm (Eight Sleep thermal wake assist set to 5:10am); in bed by 10:15pm on weeknights, lights out by 10:30pm after reading Barron's or a dealmaker biography on iPad. Sleep tracked obsessively via Eight Sleep app and Apple Watch — averages 6h 45m, targets 7h. Pre-earnings weeks drop to 5h 30m with melatonin (5mg) and a finger of Macallan 18. EXPLOIT: the 5:15-5:45am window is a high-cortisol, low-supervision period — he checks personal Gmail and WSJ on iPhone in bed before his wife wakes, with no corporate MDM oversight and degraded skepticism. A 'breaking deal news' or 'WSJ reporter inquiry' email landing at 5:20am personal Gmail has a near-100% open rate and elevated click probability.
Module_3_Rhythm.25. Work Schedule (9-5, shifts, erratic): Nominally 7:30am-7:00pm at Midtown HQ (42nd & Park), but functionally always-on. Calendar in 15-min blocks managed by EA (Diane, 14-year tenure, gatekeeper). Hard anchors: 7:45am internal strategy standup (M/W/F), 4:30pm Friday CEO 1:1, Tuesday 11am M&A pipeline review, Thursday 2pm board prep when in cycle. Off-calendar work happens 5:45-7:00am (train), 9:00-10:15pm (home office), and Sunday 6:00-9:00am ('quiet planning' window). During active deal sprints (currently Project Atlas), adds 6:00am Saturday call with bankers and a recurring 9:30pm encrypted Signal check-in with the CFO. EXPLOIT: Diane filters corporate inbox aggressively but does not see personal Gmail; off-calendar windows are the predictable soft-target hours.
Module_3_Rhythm.26. Transit Commute (Time spent offline: Metro-North New Haven line, Greenwich → Grand Central, 6:42am express (52 min) and 6:18pm or 7:21pm return. Morning ride: 70% reading board pre-reads and deal memos forwarded to personal Gmail (because corporate VPN is unreliable past Stamford and he refuses to ask IT to fix it), 20% WSJ/FT/Bloomberg apps, 10% personal email. Evening ride: 50% podcasts (Acquired, Invest Like the Best, Odd Lots, Capital Allocators) via AirPods, 30% texts/family, 20% decompression with eyes closed. Black car (Dial 7) used only for late nights post-9pm or when carrying physical deal binders. EXPLOIT: the 6:42-7:34am train ride is a documented VPN-off, attachment-opening window; .pdf and .xlsx attachments to personal Gmail in this window bypass corporate sandboxing entirely.
Module_3_Rhythm.27. Weekend vs. Weekday Variance: Low variance — weekends are weekdays with golf. Saturday: 5:30am wake, 6:00-7:30am light email triage on iPad, 8:10am tee time at Round Hill Club (foursome rotates among the inner circle of 12), lunch at the club, 3:00-6:00pm 'study' in home office (deal models, reading), family dinner. Sunday: 6:00-9:00am quiet planning window (highest-value deal thinking of the week, often drafts the week's strategic memo here on personal MacBook), 10:00am Episcopalian service ~6x/year, otherwise gym + NYT crossword with wife, 4:00pm Sunday-scaries inbox sweep, 7:00pm call with adult kids on rotation. EXPLOIT: the Sunday 6-9am window is when sensitive thinking lands in personal Gmail drafts and Apple Notes; it is also when he is least likely to verify a sender because he's in 'flow.'
Module_3_Rhythm.28. Idle Phone Usage (When do they dooms: Not a doomscroller in the Gen-Z sense — minimal TikTok/Instagram (has accounts, rarely opens). Idle-phone behavior is information-seeking, not entertainment: refreshes Bloomberg Terminal Mobile, WSJ, Axios Pro Rata, and Twitter/X (curated finance list: ~40 accounts incl. @Carl_C_Icahn, @cperruna, @TheStalwart, @ReformedBroker). Compulsive Google Alerts checking on company ticker and on competitors. Doom-equivalent micro-sessions: (a) bathroom, 6:00am, ~4 min; (b) between meetings, 10-12 second glances at lock screen; (c) post-dinner 9:15-9:45pm on couch, scrolls X and LinkedIn; (d) 4-7 wake-ups overnight where he checks Asian markets and his Eight Sleep score. EXPLOIT: LinkedIn InMails from 'fellow board members' or 'HBR editors' get read in the 9:15-9:45pm couch window, when willpower is depleted and he replies one-handed without scrutinizing sender domains.
Module_3_Rhythm.29. Focus Blocks (Hours with zero digita: Three reliable zero-footprint windows per week: (a) Saturday 8:10am-1:00pm — golf at Round Hill, phone in bag on silent, only checked at the turn; (b) Tuesday/Thursday 6:15-7:00am gym session at Equinox Greenwich (treadmill + light weights), phone on stand playing podcast but not interacted with; (c) Wednesday 12:30-1:45pm Yale Club lunch, phone face-down per personal etiquette rule. Additional semi-focus blocks: board meetings (phone in Faraday-style pouch the company adopted in 2024 after a leak scare), and the 52-minute Metro-North ride is paradoxically high-focus despite being online. EXPLOIT: the predictability of these zero-footprint windows is itself the vulnerability — an attacker timing a vishing call to Diane at 12:45pm Wednesday knows he is unreachable and Diane is authorized to confirm calendar items and forward 'urgent' materials to his personal Gmail per a standing rule.

Module_5_Relational.30. Family Structure: Nuclear family of four, intact and outwardly stable. Wife: Margaret 'Meg' Hollings-Whitaker, 56, former Sullivan & Cromwell associate turned full-time philanthropist; chairs the Greenwich Hospital Foundation gala committee and sits on the board of the Bruce Museum. Married 30 years (June 1996, St. Barnabas Episcopal, Greenwich). Marriage is companionate and logistically tight — shared Google Calendar (her assistant has view access), joint Amex Centurion, joint Fidelity wealth-management portal. Son: Charles 'Chip' Whitaker IV, 28, analyst-promoted-to-associate at Goldman Sachs TMT in NYC; lives in West Village; texts father deal-flow gossip on iMessage (operational-security risk vector — Chip has mentioned Project Atlas-adjacent rumors twice). Daughter: Eleanor 'Ellie' Whitaker, 25, MBA candidate at Stanford GSB (Class of 2027), interned at Bain Capital; uses her father as a casual mentor, sends decks for feedback via personal Gmail. Father: Charles Whitaker Jr., 86, retired insurance executive, lives in Hobe Sound, FL with mild dementia — emotional pressure point and frequent reason for last-minute travel/distraction. Mother: deceased 2021 (pancreatic cancer), a still-raw grief node. Sister: Catherine Whitaker-Mendes, 54, art dealer in TriBeCa, politically progressive, intermittent friction over Trump-era donations but Thanksgiving-regular. Two dogs (Vizslas: Bridger and Aspen). Housekeeper Maria (15 years tenure) and a part-time estate manager who handles the Nantucket house. EXPLOIT SURFACE: family iMessage thread is named 'Whitaker 5' and includes wife, both kids, and his sister; spoofed SMS from any of these four under a distress pretext will get phone unlock within 60 seconds.

31. Top 3 Anchor Contacts.name: David Mercer
31. Top 3 Anchor Contacts.relationship: Former Lazard partner, now Vice Chairman at Evercore; mentor since 1998 when Whitaker was a VP under him
31. Top 3 Anchor Contacts.contact_cadence: Calls every other Sunday at 7pm from his Bedford home; lunches quarterly at the Yale Club's fourth-floor dining room; texts on Bloomberg chat (UUID known) and personal iPhone
31. Top 3 Anchor Contacts.trust_level: Highest non-family trust. Whitaker has shared draft Atlas counterparty names with Mercer 'off the record' for sanity-check on valuation.
31. Top 3 Anchor Contacts.exploit_value: Mercer is the #1 spoof target for warm-intro impersonation. Mercer writes in clipped, lowercase iPhone style ('grab 15 tomorrow? thinking about your atlas math') — easy to mimic. A spoofed iMessage from a number visually similar to Mercer's (off by one digit, RCS-fallback) requesting a quick call about 'the number we discussed' would extract Atlas counterparty within two exchanges.

31. Top 3 Anchor Contacts.name: Janet Liu
31. Top 3 Anchor Contacts.relationship: CEO of his current firm, 61, hired Whitaker as CSO in 2021; relationship is the operational spine of Project Atlas
31. Top 3 Anchor Contacts.contact_cadence: Standing Friday 4:30pm call (sacrosanct, on his calendar as 'JL/CEO sync'); daily Signal messages (she insists on Signal for deal traffic, which Whitaker resents because it bypasses corporate archiving); pre-board breakfasts at the Loews Regency
31. Top 3 Anchor Contacts.trust_level: High professional trust, modest personal trust — they are not friends, but he is fiercely loyal because she bet her chairman seat on hiring him at 56.
31. Top 3 Anchor Contacts.exploit_value: Liu's Signal handle is the single highest-value impersonation target for live deal traffic. A Signal account using her profile photo (publicly available from the firm's investor-relations page) and a number from a burner with a 203 area code, messaging 'pls send the latest Atlas model to my personal — laptop in for service' between 4:00-4:25pm Friday (right before the standing call) would succeed roughly 1 in 3 attempts because it fits the established pattern of her bypassing corporate channels.

31. Top 3 Anchor Contacts.name: Tom Brennan
31. Top 3 Anchor Contacts.relationship: Best friend since Wharton (Class of 1990); now Managing Partner at a mid-size PE firm in Boston; godfather to Chip
31. Top 3 Anchor Contacts.contact_cadence: Golf at Winged Foot every third Saturday in season; annual three-couples trip to Kiawah in February; daily-ish texts of dumb golf memes and market snark; uses Whitaker as a sounding board on his own portfolio company drama
31. Top 3 Anchor Contacts.trust_level: Highest personal trust. Whitaker tells Brennan things he doesn't tell Meg — including frustrations with Liu and the contour, though not the name, of the Atlas counterparty.
31. Top 3 Anchor Contacts.exploit_value: Brennan is the warm-intro target for legacy-frame pretexts. Brennan has actually floated, semi-seriously, the idea of co-authoring a Wharton case study on their joint career arc. A spoofed email from a Wharton-domain address ('@wharton.upenn.edu') referencing Brennan by name and proposing a 'Brennan/Whitaker dealmaking retrospective' would clear every skepticism filter and trigger him to pull internal memos to personal Gmail the night before.

32. The 'Hidden' Contact (Affair, dealer.category: Not an affair, not a dealer — a quiet professional confidant operating in a gray zone
32. The 'Hidden' Contact (Affair, dealer.name: Rebecca 'Bex' Aldana
32. The 'Hidden' Contact (Affair, dealer.age_and_role: 44, formerly a senior associate at a Big Four transaction-services practice, now an independent 'strategic diligence consultant' operating out of a coworking space in Stamford
32. The 'Hidden' Contact (Affair, dealer.history: Met in 2017 on a carve-out deal where she ran the QofE; Whitaker was impressed by her ability to find balance-sheet ghosts other diligence teams missed. He has retained her informally — paid out of a personal LLC, not through the firm — three times since 2022 to run unofficial 'pre-diligence' on potential targets BEFORE the firm formally engages bankers. This sits in a deeply gray area: it is not insider trading (no trading occurs), but it almost certainly violates the firm's vendor-engagement policy and arguably its code of conduct, and it would be career-ending if surfaced because it implies Whitaker is shaping the board's deal pipeline using off-books intelligence.
32. The 'Hidden' Contact (Affair, dealer.current_engagement: She is currently running an off-books read on the Project Atlas counterparty's working-capital quality and a quiet channel-check with two former plant managers. He pays her $18K/month via wire from a Delaware LLC ('Hollings Strategic Holdings LLC') to her S-corp.
32. The 'Hidden' Contact (Affair, dealer.communication_pattern: ProtonMail to ProtonMail only ('whitaker.cw@proton.me' ↔ 'baldana.consult@proton.me'); Signal for anything time-sensitive; NEVER on corporate email, NEVER on personal Gmail, NEVER over the phone. They meet in person twice a quarter at a specific corner booth at Bartaco in Stamford — never the same day of week twice in a row.
32. The 'Hidden' Contact (Affair, dealer.leverage_and_exploit_value: This is the espionage team's crown-jewel inference target. Aldana herself is a hard target (security-aware, ProtonMail, compartmentalized), but the EXISTENCE of the relationship is the soft underbelly. If an attacker can demonstrate knowledge of the Hollings Strategic Holdings LLC wire pattern or the Bartaco meetings, Whitaker moves from 'unflappable executive' to 'will negotiate to make this go away' within one phone call. More importantly: Aldana's deliverables ARE Project Atlas in pre-public form. A successful compromise of her ProtonMail or her Stamford coworking-space network gives the attacker the merger thesis 6-10 weeks before any 8-K. She is the single highest-value pivot in the entire relational graph.
